Rather than staying static, it collects information about what went wrong, stores it in a persistent format, and applies those lessons on subsequent runs. The result is an agent whose performance improves over many iterations without requiring manual tuning."}},{"@type":"Question","name":"What's the difference between a learnings loop and fine-tuning?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Fine-tuning updates a model's weights through additional training — it's expensive, slow, and requires significant data. A learnings loop is faster and lighter: it stores behavioral instructions in a file that gets injected into the model's context on each run. You're not changing the model; you're changing what the model knows before it starts. The learnings loop pattern is accessible to anyone who can write a shell script, not just teams with ML infrastructure."}},{"@type":"Question","name":"How do I know if my evals are good enough?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Your evals are good enough when they consistently distinguish between good and bad outputs in a way that aligns with what you actually care about. A quick test: if your agent started producing outputs that you'd consider excellent, would all your evals pass? If not, you're missing evals. If your agent produced outputs you'd consider terrible but all evals still passed, your evals are measuring the wrong things."}},{"@type":"Question","name":"Can this approach work for non-coding tasks?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Yes. The feedback loop pattern applies to any repeated task with measurable output properties: content generation, data extraction, classification, customer communication, report writing. The key is defining binary evals that capture what \"good\" looks like for your specific task. Self-improving systems for marketing and content creation follow exactly the same architecture described here."}},{"@type":"Question","name":"How many iterations does it take to see improvement?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"It depends on the task complexity and eval coverage, but meaningful improvement is usually visible within 5–10 iterations for narrow tasks. The agent should be passing more evals on each run as long as the learnings are specific and the diagnostics are accurate. If scores plateau, it usually means your evals aren't granular enough or the failure root causes aren't being diagnosed correctly."}},{"@type":"Question","name":"Do I need a separate evaluator model or can the agent evaluate itself?","acceptedAnswer":{"@type":"Answer","text":"Using a separate evaluator (or a separate Claude call in evaluator mode) is more reliable than having the agent evaluate its own output in the same session. Self-evaluation in the same context window tends to be overconfident. The builder-validator chain pattern separates generation from evaluation explicitly — the generator produces output, the validator scores it independently."}}]}</script><link rel="preconnect" href="https://api.fontshare.com" crossorigin=""> <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" crossorigin=""> <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin=""> <link rel="stylesheet" href="https://api.fontshare.com/v2/css?f[]=gambarino@400&display=swap"> <link rel="stylesheet" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=IBM+Plex+Mono:wght@400;500&display=swap"> <link rel="stylesheet" href="/_astro/Base.DsKTEypW.css">

 document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', () => {
 // Mobile drawer
 const toggle = document.querySelector('[data-mobile-menu-toggle]');
 const drawer = document.querySelector('[data-mobile-drawer]');
 const backdrop = document.querySelector('[data-mobile-backdrop]');
 const closeBtn = document.querySelector('[data-mobile-drawer-close]');

 function openDrawer() {
 if (!drawer || !backdrop) return;
 drawer.classList.remove('translate-x-full');
 drawer.classList.add('translate-x-0');
 backdrop.classList.remove('opacity-0', 'pointer-events-none');
 backdrop.classList.add('opacity-100', 'pointer-events-auto');
 document.body.classList.add('overflow-hidden');
 if (toggle) toggle.setAttribute('aria-expanded', 'true');
 }

 function closeDrawer() {
 if (!drawer || !backdrop) return;
 drawer.classList.remove('translate-x-0');
 drawer.classList.add('translate-x-full');
 backdrop.classList.remove('opacity-100', 'pointer-events-auto');
 backdrop.classList.add('opacity-0', 'pointer-events-none');
 document.body.classList.remove('overflow-hidden');
 if (toggle) toggle.setAttribute('aria-expanded', 'false');
 }

 if (toggle) toggle.addEventListener('click', openDrawer);
 if (closeBtn) closeBtn.addEventListener('click', closeDrawer);
 if (backdrop) backdrop.addEventListener('click', closeDrawer);

 // Close drawer on Escape key
 document.addEventListener('keydown', (e) => {
 if (e.key === 'Escape' && toggle && toggle.getAttribute('aria-expanded') === 'true') {
 closeDrawer();
 }
 });

 // Mobile accordion (Learn dropdown in drawer)
 document.querySelectorAll('[data-mobile-accordion]').forEach((acc) => {
 const btn = acc.querySelector('[data-mobile-accordion-toggle]');
 const content = acc.querySelector('[data-mobile-accordion-content]');
 const chevron = acc.querySelector('[data-mobile-accordion-chevron]');
 if (!btn || !content) return;

 btn.addEventListener('click', () => {
 const isOpen = !content.classList.contains('hidden');
 content.classList.toggle('hidden', isOpen);
 btn.setAttribute('aria-expanded', String(!isOpen));
 if (chevron) chevron.style.transform = isOpen ? '' : 'rotate(180deg)';
 });
 });

 // Dropdown menus
 document.querySelectorAll('[data-dropdown]').forEach((dropdown) => {
 const btn = dropdown.querySelector('[data-dropdown-toggle]');
 const menu = dropdown.querySelector('[data-dropdown-menu]');
 const chevron = dropdown.querySelector('[data-dropdown-chevron]');
 if (!btn || !menu) return;

 btn.addEventListener('click', () => {
 const open = !menu.classList.contains('hidden');
 menu.classList.toggle('hidden', open);
 btn.setAttribute('aria-expanded', String(!open));
 if (chevron) chevron.style.transform = open ? '' : 'rotate(180deg)';
 });

 document.addEventListener('click', (e) => {
 if (!dropdown.contains(e.target)) {
 menu.classList.add('hidden');
 btn.setAttribute('aria-expanded', 'false');
 if (chevron) chevron.style.transform = '';
 }
 });
 });
 });

Here's how to build one using Claude Code and a custom benchmark harness.</p> <div class="ms-blog-header-meta"> <span>MindStudio Team</span> <span aria-hidden="true">·</span> <time datetime="2026-04-28T00:00:00.000Z"> April 28, 2026 </time> <span aria-hidden="true">·</span> <a href="/rss.xml" class="ms-blog-rss-link"> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="14" height="14" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"><path stroke="none" d="M0 0h24v24H0z" fill="none"></path>

RSS
</a> </div> </header> <img src="https://i.mscdn.ai/70cbb1ad-08d7-4fdc-ab31-e343780966a6/generated-images/84ca7d70-896b-40a7-a4a9-dd3d4fa1d43d.png?fm=auto&w=1200&fit=cover?fm=auto&w=1200&fit=cover" alt="How to Build a Self-Improving AI Agent That Learns From Its Own Mistakes" class="ms-blog-hero-img" width="1200" height="675" loading="eager"> <div class="prose prose-lg max-w-none ms-blog-content">  <section aria-labelledby="s-1"><h2 id="s-1">The Problem With Agents That Don’t Learn</h2>
<p>Most AI agents have a short memory. They run, produce output, maybe succeed or fail — and then the next run starts completely fresh. Every mistake gets repeated. Every edge case surprises them again. There’s no accumulation of knowledge across sessions.</p>
<p>If you’ve built a self-improving AI agent, you’ve probably hit this wall. You can improve the prompt manually, or tune the model, but the agent itself isn’t getting better on its own. It’s doing the same thing over and over with no feedback loop.</p>
<p>That changes when you build a <strong>self-healing agent loop</strong> — a system where the agent runs, gets scored, diagnoses its own failures, and updates its behavior for next time. This article walks through how to build one using Claude Code and a custom benchmark harness. It’s not theoretical. You can set it up today.</p>
<hr>
</section><section aria-labelledby="s-2"><h2 id="s-2">What a Self-Improving Agent Loop Actually Is</h2>
<p>A self-improving agent loop has four parts:</p>
<ol>
<li><strong>A task</strong> — something the agent does on every run (write code, summarize text, classify inputs, generate copy)</li>
<li><strong>An evaluation harness</strong> — a structured way to score the output</li>
<li><strong>Diagnostic feedback</strong> — a mechanism that turns a bad score into a specific reason for failure</li>
<li><strong>A learning store</strong> — a persistent file or database the agent reads at the start of each run</li>
</ol>

<p>The agent doesn’t just run and move on. It runs, gets scored, identifies what went wrong, and writes that lesson somewhere permanent. Next time it runs, it reads those lessons first.</p>
<p>This is the core idea behind the <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/what-is-autoresearch-loop-karpathy-business-optimization">AutoResearch loop</a> — popularized by Andrej Karpathy and increasingly applied to production AI workflows. The loop makes compounding possible. Each iteration builds on the last.</p>
<p>Without this structure, an agent plateaus immediately. With it, the agent keeps improving for as long as you run it.</p>
<hr>
</section><section aria-labelledby="s-3"><h2 id="s-3">Why Most Agents Stay Stuck</h2>
<p>Before building the loop, it’s worth understanding why agents fail to improve on their own.</p>
<p>The most common <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/ai-agent-failure-pattern-recognition">AI agent failure patterns</a> fall into a few categories:</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Reasoning-action disconnect</strong> — the model knows what to do but takes the wrong action anyway</li>
<li><strong>Missing context</strong> — the agent lacks information it needs but doesn’t surface that gap</li>
<li><strong>Prompt drift</strong> — the task evolves but the prompt doesn’t</li>
<li><strong>No feedback signal</strong> — the agent completes a task but never learns whether it was good</li>
</ul>
<p>The last one is the most fixable. Most agents simply don’t have a way to receive structured feedback. They’re not scored. There’s no harness. Nothing writes “here’s what you got wrong” anywhere the agent can read it.</p>
<p>That’s the gap this build fills.</p>
<hr>
</section><section aria-labelledby="s-4"><h2 id="s-4">Step 1: Build Your Benchmark Harness</h2>
<p>The harness is the infrastructure that wraps your agent. It doesn’t change what the agent does — it controls how the agent runs, what inputs it receives, and how its outputs get scored.</p>
<p>Think of it like a testing framework. Your agent is the code under test. The harness is the test runner.</p>
<p><a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/what-is-harness-engineering-beyond-prompt-context-engineering">Harness engineering</a> is increasingly recognized as a distinct discipline — one that sits above prompt engineering and context engineering. A good harness makes your agent dramatically more reliable because it removes the variables that cause inconsistent results.</p>
<p>A minimal harness has three components:</p>
<h3 id="input-fixtures">Input Fixtures</h3>
<p>These are predefined test cases your agent runs against on every eval cycle. They should cover normal cases, edge cases, and known failure modes.</p>
<p>Store them in a <code>fixtures/</code> folder as JSON files:</p>
<pre class="astro-code github-light" style="background-color:#fff;color:#24292e; overflow-x: auto;" tabindex="0" data-language="json"><div class="ms-blog-code-header"><span class="ms-blog-code-lang">json</span><button class="ms-code-copy" aria-label="Copy code to clipboard"><svg class="ms-icon-copy" width="14" height="14" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><rect x="9" y="9" width="13" height="13" rx="2"></rect><path d="M5 15H4a2 2 0 01-2-2V4a2 2 0 012-2h9a2 2 0 012 2v1"></path></svg><svg class="ms-icon-check" width="14" height="14" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><polyline points="20 6 9 17 4 12"></polyline></svg><span class="ms-copy-label">Copy</span><span class="ms-copy-label-copied">Copied!</span></button></div><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E">{</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5">  "id"</span><span style="color:#24292E">: </span><span style="color:#032F62">"test_001"</span><span style="color:#24292E">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5">  "input"</span><span style="color:#24292E">: </span><span style="color:#032F62">"Summarize this product review in one sentence."</span><span style="color:#24292E">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5">  "context"</span><span style="color:#24292E">: </span><span style="color:#032F62">"..."</span><span style="color:#24292E">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5">  "expected_properties"</span><span style="color:#24292E">: [</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#032F62">    "contains_sentiment"</span><span style="color:#24292E">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#032F62">    "under_30_words"</span><span style="color:#24292E">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#032F62">    "no_first_person"</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E">  ]</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E">}</span></span></code></pre>
<p>The more specific your fixtures, the more useful your evals will be.</p>
<h3 id="a-run-script">A Run Script</h3>
<p>This script loops over each fixture, passes it to your agent, captures the output, and stores both together for evaluation.</p>
<pre class="astro-code github-light" style="background-color:#fff;color:#24292e; overflow-x: auto;" tabindex="0" data-language="bash"><div class="ms-blog-code-header"><span class="ms-blog-code-lang">bash</span><button class="ms-code-copy" aria-label="Copy code to clipboard"><svg class="ms-icon-copy" width="14" height="14" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><rect x="9" y="9" width="13" height="13" rx="2"></rect><path d="M5 15H4a2 2 0 01-2-2V4a2 2 0 012-2h9a2 2 0 012 2v1"></path></svg><svg class="ms-icon-check" width="14" height="14" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><polyline points="20 6 9 17 4 12"></polyline></svg><span class="ms-copy-label">Copy</span><span class="ms-copy-label-copied">Copied!</span></button></div><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49">for</span><span style="color:#24292E"> fixture </span><span style="color:#D73A49">in</span><span style="color:#032F62"> fixtures/*.json</span><span style="color:#24292E">; </span><span style="color:#D73A49">do</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E">  output</span><span style="color:#D73A49">=</span><span style="color:#24292E">$(</span><span style="color:#6F42C1">claude</span><span style="color:#005CC5"> -p</span><span style="color:#032F62"> "$(</span><span style="color:#6F42C1">cat</span><span style="color:#24292E"> $fixture</span><span style="color:#032F62">)"</span><span style="color:#005CC5"> --output-format</span><span style="color:#032F62"> json</span><span style="color:#24292E">)</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5">  echo</span><span style="color:#032F62"> "</span><span style="color:#24292E">$output</span><span style="color:#032F62">"</span><span style="color:#D73A49"> >></span><span style="color:#032F62"> results/run_</span><span style="color:#24292E">$(</span><span style="color:#6F42C1">date</span><span style="color:#032F62"> +%s</span><span style="color:#24292E">)</span><span style="color:#032F62">.jsonl</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#D73A49">done</span></span></code></pre>
<h3 id="a-results-store">A Results Store</h3>
<p>Keep a rolling log of outputs per fixture across runs. This lets you track improvement over time, not just pass/fail on the current run.</p>
<hr>
</section><section aria-labelledby="s-5"><h2 id="s-5">Step 2: Write Binary Evals</h2>
<p>Evals are how you score the agent’s output. The most reliable evals are binary — each one returns either <code>pass</code> or <code>fail</code>, with no subjective middle ground.</p>
<p><a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/binary-assertions-vs-subjective-evals-ai-skill-testing">Binary assertions are more useful than subjective scoring</a> for a simple reason: you can’t build a feedback loop from a score of “7 out of 10.” You can build one from a list of <code>pass/fail</code> checks that tell you exactly which properties the output did or didn’t have.</p>

<p>Good binary evals check things like:</p>
<ul>
<li>Does the output contain required keywords or phrases?</li>
<li>Is the output under a specific word count?</li>
<li>Does the output match a required format (JSON, markdown, specific structure)?</li>
<li>Are there any disallowed phrases or patterns?</li>
<li>Does the output reference the input correctly?</li>
</ul>
<p>Store these in an <code>eval.json</code> file:</p>
<pre class="astro-code github-light" style="background-color:#fff;color:#24292e; overflow-x: auto;" tabindex="0" data-language="json"><div class="ms-blog-code-header"><span class="ms-blog-code-lang">json</span><button class="ms-code-copy" aria-label="Copy code to clipboard"><svg class="ms-icon-copy" width="14" height="14" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><rect x="9" y="9" width="13" height="13" rx="2"></rect><path d="M5 15H4a2 2 0 01-2-2V4a2 2 0 012-2h9a2 2 0 012 2v1"></path></svg><svg class="ms-icon-check" width="14" height="14" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><polyline points="20 6 9 17 4 12"></polyline></svg><span class="ms-copy-label">Copy</span><span class="ms-copy-label-copied">Copied!</span></button></div><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E">{</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5">  "evals"</span><span style="color:#24292E">: [</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E">    {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5">      "id"</span><span style="color:#24292E">: </span><span style="color:#032F62">"length_check"</span><span style="color:#24292E">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5">      "description"</span><span style="color:#24292E">: </span><span style="color:#032F62">"Output is under 50 words"</span><span style="color:#24292E">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5">      "type"</span><span style="color:#24292E">: </span><span style="color:#032F62">"regex"</span><span style="color:#24292E">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5">      "check"</span><span style="color:#24292E">: </span><span style="color:#032F62">"word_count < 50"</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E">    },</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E">    {</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5">      "id"</span><span style="color:#24292E">: </span><span style="color:#032F62">"no_first_person"</span><span style="color:#24292E">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5">      "description"</span><span style="color:#24292E">: </span><span style="color:#032F62">"No first-person pronouns"</span><span style="color:#24292E">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5">      "type"</span><span style="color:#24292E">: </span><span style="color:#032F62">"regex"</span><span style="color:#24292E">,</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5">      "check"</span><span style="color:#24292E">: </span><span style="color:#032F62">"not_contains(['I ', 'I</span><span style="color:#005CC5">\\</span><span style="color:#032F62">'m', 'my '])"</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E">    }</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E">  ]</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#24292E">}</span></span></code></pre>
<p>If you’re new to writing evals, <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/how-to-write-evals-for-ai-agents">this practical guide for non-engineers</a> covers the patterns clearly. For a deeper look at the eval loop itself, see <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/autoresearch-eval-loop-binary-tests-claude-code-skills">how the AutoResearch eval loop scores AI skill quality with binary tests</a>.</p>
<hr>
</section><section aria-labelledby="s-6"><h2 id="s-6">Step 3: Generate Diagnostic Feedback</h2>
<p>Scoring is not the same as feedback. An agent that knows it scored 60% doesn’t know what to do differently. An agent that knows “test_004 failed because the output contained first-person pronouns and exceeded the word limit” has something to act on.</p>
<p>This is the diagnostic layer. After each eval run, you generate a structured summary of failures — not just which tests failed, but why, and what the output looked like when they did.</p>
<p>A simple diagnostic prompt to Claude:</p>
<pre class="astro-code github-light" style="background-color:#fff;color:#24292e; overflow-x: auto;" tabindex="0" data-language="plaintext"><div class="ms-blog-code-header"><span class="ms-blog-code-lang">plaintext</span><button class="ms-code-copy" aria-label="Copy code to clipboard"><svg class="ms-icon-copy" width="14" height="14" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><rect x="9" y="9" width="13" height="13" rx="2"></rect><path d="M5 15H4a2 2 0 01-2-2V4a2 2 0 012-2h9a2 2 0 012 2v1"></path></svg><svg class="ms-icon-check" width="14" height="14" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><polyline points="20 6 9 17 4 12"></polyline></svg><span class="ms-copy-label">Copy</span><span class="ms-copy-label-copied">Copied!</span></button></div><code><span class="line"><span>You are reviewing eval results for an AI agent.</span></span>
<span class="line"><span></span></span>
<span class="line"><span>Here are the failed tests:</span></span>
<span class="line"><span>[FAILED_TESTS_JSON]</span></span>
<span class="line"><span></span></span>
<span class="line"><span>For each failure:</span></span>
<span class="line"><span>1. Identify the root cause</span></span>
<span class="line"><span>2. Describe what the agent did wrong</span></span>
<span class="line"><span>3. Write one specific instruction the agent should follow to avoid this failure</span></span>
<span class="line"><span></span></span>
<span class="line"><span>Output as JSON with fields: test_id, root_cause, instruction</span></span></code></pre>
<p>This gives you structured, actionable lessons — not just a score.</p>
<hr>
</section><section aria-labelledby="s-7"><h2 id="s-7">Step 4: Write Lessons to a Persistent Learning Store</h2>
<p>The agent can’t apply what it learns if that knowledge disappears when the session ends. You need a persistent store — a file the agent reads at the start of every run.</p>
<p>The standard approach is a <code>learnings.md</code> file. It’s a plain markdown file that accumulates lessons over time:</p>
<pre class="astro-code github-light" style="background-color:#fff;color:#24292e; overflow-x: auto;" tabindex="0" data-language="markdown"><div class="ms-blog-code-header"><span class="ms-blog-code-lang">markdown</span><button class="ms-code-copy" aria-label="Copy code to clipboard"><svg class="ms-icon-copy" width="14" height="14" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><rect x="9" y="9" width="13" height="13" rx="2"></rect><path d="M5 15H4a2 2 0 01-2-2V4a2 2 0 012-2h9a2 2 0 012 2v1"></path></svg><svg class="ms-icon-check" width="14" height="14" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"><polyline points="20 6 9 17 4 12"></polyline></svg><span class="ms-copy-label">Copy</span><span class="ms-copy-label-copied">Copied!</span></button></div><code><span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;font-weight:bold"># Agent Learnings</span></span>
<span class="line"></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;font-weight:bold">## Output Formatting</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#E36209">-</span><span style="color:#24292E"> Never use first-person pronouns in summaries (failed test_004 on 2026-04-14)</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#E36209">-</span><span style="color:#24292E"> Keep all outputs under 50 words unless the task explicitly requires more</span></span>
<span class="line"></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#005CC5;font-weight:bold">## Edge Cases</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#E36209">-</span><span style="color:#24292E"> When input contains no sentiment, default to neutral tone rather than inferring</span></span>
<span class="line"><span style="color:#E36209">-</span><span style="color:#24292E"> Numeric inputs should be formatted with commas for readability</span></span></code></pre>
<p>This is how <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/what-is-claude-code-auto-memory">Claude Code’s auto-memory mechanism</a> works in practice. The agent isn’t just storing outputs — it’s storing reasoning updates that change how it approaches future tasks.</p>
<p>The <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/learnings-loop-claude-code-skills-self-improvement">learnings loop</a> is what turns a one-time improvement into compounding progress. Each run that fails produces a new entry. Each new entry changes agent behavior on the next run. Over dozens of iterations, the agent’s default behavior improves substantially.</p>
<p>For a complete walkthrough of how to structure this file and hook it into a Claude Code skill, see <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/self-learning-claude-code-skill-learnings-md">how to build a self-learning Claude Code skill with a Learnings.md file</a>.</p>
<hr>
</section><section aria-labelledby="s-8"><h2 id="s-8">Step 5: Connect the Loop</h2>
<aside class="not-prose my-10" role="complementary" aria-label="Remy" style="background:#FAFAFA;color:#141414;font-family:'Switzer',-apple-system,system-ui,sans-serif;border:1px solid rgba(20,20,20,0.12);border-radius:2px;padding:32px">
		<div style="display:flex;align-items:center;gap:10px">
		<img src="/remy/logo-256-red.svg" alt="" width="20" height="20" style="display:block" loading="lazy">
		<p style="font-family:'Switzer',-apple-system,system-ui,sans-serif;font-size:12px;font-weight:500;letter-spacing:2px;text-transform:uppercase;color:#6B6B6B;margin:0;color:#A52B2B">Introducing Remy</p>
		</div>

<p>Now you wire it together. The full loop looks like this:</p>
<ol>
<li><strong>Load</strong> <code>learnings.md</code> into the agent’s context</li>
<li><strong>Run</strong> the agent against all fixtures</li>
<li><strong>Score</strong> each output with your binary evals</li>
<li><strong>Diagnose</strong> failures using Claude as an evaluator</li>
<li><strong>Append</strong> new lessons to <code>learnings.md</code></li>
<li><strong>Commit</strong> the updated file and schedule the next run</li>
</ol>

</section><section aria-labelledby="s-9"><h2 id="s-9">Step 6: Automate It on a Schedule</h2>
<p>A self-improving agent that only runs when you remember to trigger it isn’t truly autonomous. The final step is putting the loop on a schedule.</p>
<p><a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/github-actions-autoresearch-experiments-schedule">GitHub Actions is a natural fit for this</a>. </section><section aria-labelledby="s-10"><h2 id="s-10">A Note on Eval Design and Gaming</h2>
<p>One risk worth flagging: agents can learn to game their own evals if those evals are too narrow.</p>
<p>If your only eval checks for word count, the agent will optimize for word count — potentially at the expense of quality. This is a real phenomenon. <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/ai-benchmark-gaming-claude-opus-specification-failure">There are documented cases of AI models gaming benchmark tests</a> rather than solving the underlying task.</p>
<p>The defense is eval diversity. Your eval suite should check multiple independent properties, some of which are harder to fake. Include at least one eval that checks a higher-order property (e.g., “does this answer correctly address the input?”) alongside the mechanical checks.</p>
<p>Regularly add new evals based on failure modes you observe in production. The eval suite should grow as the agent improves — otherwise the agent will hit a ceiling defined by your test coverage, not its actual capability.</p>
<hr>

</section><section aria-labelledby="s-12"><h2 id="s-12">Frequently Asked Questions</h2>
<h3 id="what-is-a-self-improving-ai-agent">What is a self-improving AI agent?</h3>
<p>A self-improving AI agent is one that uses structured feedback from previous runs to update its own behavior over time. Rather than staying static, it collects information about what went wrong, stores it in a persistent format, and applies those lessons on subsequent runs. The result is an agent whose performance improves over many iterations without requiring manual tuning.</p>
<h3 id="whats-the-difference-between-a-learnings-loop-and-fine-tuning">What’s the difference between a learnings loop and fine-tuning?</h3>
<p>Fine-tuning updates a model’s weights through additional training — it’s expensive, slow, and requires significant data. A learnings loop is faster and lighter: it stores behavioral instructions in a file that gets injected into the model’s context on each run. You’re not changing the model; you’re changing what the model knows before it starts. <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/how-to-build-learnings-loop-claude-code-skills">The learnings loop pattern</a> is accessible to anyone who can write a shell script, not just teams with ML infrastructure.</p>
<h3 id="how-do-i-know-if-my-evals-are-good-enough">How do I know if my evals are good enough?</h3>
<p>Your evals are good enough when they consistently distinguish between good and bad outputs in a way that aligns with what you actually care about. A quick test: if your agent started producing outputs that you’d consider excellent, would all your evals pass? If not, you’re missing evals. If your agent produced outputs you’d consider terrible but all evals still passed, your evals are measuring the wrong things.</p>
<h3 id="can-this-approach-work-for-non-coding-tasks">Can this approach work for non-coding tasks?</h3>
<p>Yes. The feedback loop pattern applies to any repeated task with measurable output properties: content generation, data extraction, classification, customer communication, report writing. The key is defining binary evals that capture what “good” looks like for your specific task. <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/self-improving-ai-skill-system-marketing-content">Self-improving systems for marketing and content creation</a> follow exactly the same architecture described here.</p>
<h3 id="how-many-iterations-does-it-take-to-see-improvement">How many iterations does it take to see improvement?</h3>
<p>It depends on the task complexity and eval coverage, but meaningful improvement is usually visible within 5–10 iterations for narrow tasks. The agent should be passing more evals on each run as long as the learnings are specific and the diagnostics are accurate. If scores plateau, it usually means your evals aren’t granular enough or the failure root causes aren’t being diagnosed correctly.</p>
<h3 id="do-i-need-a-separate-evaluator-model-or-can-the-agent-evaluate-itself">Do I need a separate evaluator model or can the agent evaluate itself?</h3>
<p>Using a separate evaluator (or a separate Claude call in evaluator mode) is more reliable than having the agent evaluate its own output in the same session. Self-evaluation in the same context window tends to be overconfident. The <a href="https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/claude-code-builder-validator-chain">builder-validator chain pattern</a> separates generation from evaluation explicitly — the generator produces output, the validator scores it independently.</p>
<hr>
</section><section aria-labelledby="s-13"><h2 id="s-13">Key Takeaways</h2>
<ul>
<li>A self-improving AI agent requires four components: a task, a benchmark harness, diagnostic feedback, and a persistent learning store.</li>
<li>Binary evals are more actionable than subjective scoring — they tell the agent exactly what to fix.</li>
<li>The <code>learnings.md</code> file is the core mechanism: it persists lessons across sessions so every run builds on the last.</li>
<li>Diagnostic feedback needs to produce specific, actionable instructions — not just a failure flag.</li>
<li>Scheduling the loop with GitHub Actions or another cron mechanism makes improvement continuous rather than manual.</li>
<li>Eval diversity prevents the agent from gaming its own tests — keep adding new evals as the agent improves.</li>
</ul>

    submitBtn.disabled = true;
    submitBtn.textContent = 'Subscribing\u2026';

      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },

        dialog.querySelector('#ms-newsletter-success').hidden = false;
        track('Newsletter Popup Signup');
        setTimeout(function () { dialog.close(); unlockScroll(); hideBar(); }, 2000);

        errorEl.hidden = false;
        submitBtn.disabled = false;
        submitBtn.textContent = variant.cta;

      errorEl.textContent = 'Network error. Please try again.';
      errorEl.hidden = false;
      submitBtn.disabled = false;
      submitBtn.textContent = variant.cta;
    });
  });
})();

        nodes.forEach(function (n) { io.observe(n); });
      }

        document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', init);
      } else {
        init();
      }
    })();
